frontmatter.title: External References
frontmatter.description: External references dynamically include content into your MyST Markdown projects, from Wikipedia, intersphinx, DOIs, RRIDs, and any other MyST projects. Allowing your documents to be rich, interactive and automatically kept up to date.
frontmatter.subject: MyST Markdown

children.value: Markdown allows you to connect your

-references with external content.
External references are references to structured content or documents that are outside of your project.
supports referencing rich content in a growing number of formats, including:
projects, with rich cross-linking of content
showing structured content from scholarly sources like
for referencing content in your project and
to cite scholarly work and create bibliographies.

is provided for the deployed site.
This provides pre-parsed, structured content as an
that can be included in other projects and rendered in a tooltip.

pages come with a structured data representation that provides authors, license information,
as well as the full content in a parsed form that can be used for an inline reference on external pages.

In your project configuration, include the
object with links out to the external

projects that you will reference in your project. The keys in this object have no special meaning; they are just used within your project to look up the external project. The example below shows how you would load cross-references that are pulled from the

will then provide access to all of content on those pages in links and embeds.
When you specify these in your project configuration,

will load the cross-references and provide access to all of the pages and reference targets in that project.
References are cached to disk locally in the

folder, eliminating duplicate web requests on subsequent builds.

The target is everything that follows the
and is a named reference in the project.
In the example above it is “library/abc”.
As with any link, the text can be overridden using markdown link syntax
How to find the Sphinx target?
s that are part of the documentation are not always the targets that are used in the documentation. The easiest way to find the target to use is to look at the source documentation in
will warn you in the console if your target is not found.

>> intersphinx list https://docs.python.org/3.13 --domain std:doc --includes abc --limit 5

std:doc Abstract Base Classes (library/abc)
  https://docs.python.org/3.13/library/abc.html
std:doc Abstract Base Classes for Containers (library/collections.abc)
  https://docs.python.org/3.13/library/collections.abc.html
Use the target in the parenthesis, which would be
to create hover-card information directly integrated into your myst documents. The syntax follows standard markdown links, under the
protocol followed by the page title
. As with any other link, you can either follow a
, which if no text is provided for the links will be replaced with the page title.
Primordial <wiki:gravitational_waves> are hypothesized to arise from <wiki:cosmic_inflation>, a faster-than-light expansion just after the <wiki:big_bang>.
The links will take you to Wikipedia, as well as provide a tooltip and description directly on the page.
To show different text you can use a similar technique to references:
Finding and formatting the page title
To find the page title, browse Wikipedia and copy the last part of the
. If you do not supply text for the link specifically, then the case of the link will be preserved and shown without the underscores.
Usually the page titles resolve properly, so just try guessing when you are writing and then you can check them with the live hover preview.
Note that if the page title has spaces in it, simply replace them with underscores.
There are many different official and unofficial wikis that use the same
technology, including subdomains in various languages.
will work fine out of the box, and point to

Markdown can directly link and show preview of GitHub issues and Pull Requests, for example,
. To enable this, just use a normal link to your
If you do not include children for the link, then the default text will become
Markdown can directly integrate with links to GitHub to create hover-card information directly integrated into your
documents. For example, a link to the
plugin code shows a preview of the code. The code preview works for both multiple line numbers and highlighting
, which shows the surrounding ten lines, with the referenced line highlighted. If you reference the
then the first ten lines of the file are shown in the preview.
GitHub links to code can be generated on the GitHub web application when browsing code and click on the line numbers, then copy the
. To select multiple lines, click your first line then shift-click to select multiple lines, the
will be updated to end with
. The structure of the link should look like:
will work, however, picking a branch like
may mean that your code line numbers will change, instead, you may want to go to navigate to a specific git commit or tag, which will show up in the

s are persistent, unique identifiers for referencing a research resource, such as an antibody, plasmid, organism, or scientific tool. These are helpful for ensuring reproducibility and exact communication in scientific studies. See the
Markdown allows you to directly integrate with the
database to pull information and validate the links are correct as you are writing documents. The metadata is passed to subsequent systems (e.g.
documents, compatible journals and preprint servers) and helps keep your science reproducible.
protocol followed by the resource identifier, for example:
Impaired speed encoding and grid cell periodicity in a mouse model of tauopathy
s below to see additional metadata!
) is a global, community-led registry of open persistent identifiers for research organizations. You can add these to your
frontmatter or use the links directly in your documents.
protocol followed by the identifier, for example:
. To find your organization use the search provided at
Replace any spaces in the page title with underscores.
